title: Topic 406: The Psychology of Social Impact Games: Changing Behavior Through Play
content:
_ABSTRACT_

This paper embarks on a quixotic quest to understand a peculiar species of digital entertainment: the social impact game. These are not your garden-variety, dragon-slaying, princess-saving video games. No, these are games with a conscience, games that want to make you a better person, whether you like it or not. We delve into the psychological trickery and behavioral voodoo that these games employ to make us care about everything from global poverty to the plight of the humble honeybee. Through a series of case studies that range from the genuinely life-saving to the hilariously misguided, we explore how developers are harnessing the power of play to change the world, one button-mash at a time. We conclude that while social impact games may not be the answer to all of life’s problems, they are a fascinating and increasingly influential new frontier in the age-old art of telling people what to do.

_INTRODUCTION_

Once upon a time, in a kingdom not so far away, video games were simple. You were a plumber, you jumped on turtles, you saved a princess. The stakes were low, the pixels were chunky, and the most profound moral dilemma you faced was whether to sacrifice a life for a 1-Up mushroom. But somewhere along the way, a strange and wonderful thing happened. Video games grew up. They got a conscience. They started to care.

Welcome, dear reader, to the weird and wonderful world of social impact games. These are the games that want to do more than just entertain you; they want to change you. They want to make you a better citizen, a more empathetic human being, a more conscientious consumer. They want you to save the rainforest, solve the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, and understand the struggles of a Syrian refugee, all from the comfort of your ergonomic gaming chair. It’s a noble, if slightly audacious, goal. Can a video game really change the world? Or is this just another case of Silicon Valley solutionism, a well-intentioned but ultimately naive attempt to solve complex social problems with a bit of code and a catchy soundtrack?

This paper will argue that the answer, like a particularly tricky boss battle, is complicated. We will explore the psychological principles that underpin the effectiveness of social impact games, from the empathy-building power of perspective-taking to the motivational pull of gamified altruism. We will examine the evidence, both anecdotal and empirical, for the real-world impact of these games, from the life-saving success of “Re-Mission” to the cautionary tale of “SPENT.” And we will do it all with a healthy dose of humor and a critical eye, because if there’s one thing that’s more fun than playing a video game, it’s dissecting it with the precision of a stand-up comedian and the intellectual rigor of a tenured professor. So grab your controller, suspend your disbelief, and prepare to enter a world where play is serious business.

_LITERATURE REVIEW_

The notion that games can be more than mere diversion is not, in itself, a novel concept. For decades, educators and researchers have been exploring the potential of “serious games” to teach, train, and inform. But it is only in recent years, with the ubiquity of digital technology and the rise of a new generation of socially conscious developers, that the field of social impact gaming has truly come into its own. The literature on the subject is a motley crew of academic studies, industry reports, and journalistic think pieces, all grappling with the same fundamental question: can a game make a difference?

One of the earliest and most-cited examples of a successful social impact game is “Re-Mission,” a third-person shooter developed by the non-profit organization HopeLab. The game, designed for teenagers and young adults with cancer, puts players in control of a nanobot that battles cancer cells within the human body. A 2008 study published in the journal *Pediatrics* found that players of “Re-Mission” were significantly more likely to adhere to their prescribed medication regimens than a control group. The game, it seemed, had managed to do what countless doctors and parents had failed to do: make taking your medicine cool. Or, at the very least, less of a drag.

Another landmark in the field is “PeaceMaker,” a game that tasks players with resolving the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. As either the Israeli Prime Minister or the Palestinian President, players must navigate the treacherous political landscape, making decisions that have far-reaching consequences. A 2015 study in *Computers in Human Behavior* found that playing “PeaceMaker” could lead to a more nuanced understanding of the conflict and even a shift in players’ political attitudes. The game, it turned out, was a surprisingly effective tool for fostering empathy and perspective-taking, a feat that has eluded diplomats and world leaders for decades.

More recently, the concept of “gamification” has taken the world by storm, with everyone from corporate marketers to public health officials jumping on the bandwagon. The idea is simple: take the elements that make games so engaging—points, badges, leaderboards—and apply them to real-world activities. The result is a world in which we are constantly being nudged, prodded, and rewarded for our behavior, whether it’s choosing the healthy option at the supermarket or recycling our empty soda cans. A 2022 scientometric review in *Acta Psychologica* of nearly 1,000 publications on the topic concluded that gamification is a powerful tool for behavior change, with applications in a wide range of domains. The review also noted, with a hint of academic understatement, that the field is still in its infancy and that more research is needed to fully understand the long-term effects of turning our lives into a giant video game. One can only hope that future research will also address the existential dread that comes with realizing that your every move is being tracked, scored, and compared to your friends’.

_ANALYSIS_

So, what is the secret sauce? What is the psychological alchemy that allows a seemingly frivolous video game to change the way we think, feel, and act? The answer, it turns out, is a complex cocktail of cognitive biases, emotional triggers, and motivational levers, all cleverly disguised as a fun and engaging interactive experience.

At the heart of many social impact games is the principle of **empathy**. By putting players in the shoes of another person, these games can foster a deeper understanding of their experiences and perspectives. “Auti-Sim,” for example, simulates the sensory overload that a person with autism might experience in a crowded playground. The result is a disorienting and uncomfortable experience that is, by all accounts, not very “fun.” But it is also a powerful tool for building empathy, for allowing players to glimpse the world through another’s eyes. Similarly, “Never Alone,” a game developed in collaboration with the Iñupiat people of Alaska, immerses players in the rich storytelling traditions of a culture that is often misunderstood or ignored. By weaving together gameplay and cultural heritage, the game creates a powerful sense of connection and shared humanity.

But empathy is a tricky business, and not all attempts to engineer it are successful. The case of “SPENT,” a game designed to simulate the experience of poverty, is a cautionary tale. The game, which challenges players to make a series of difficult financial decisions, was intended to foster empathy for those living in poverty. But a 2016 study in the journal *Cyberpsychology* found that for some players, the game had the opposite effect. By putting players in a position of control, the game inadvertently reinforced the belief that poverty is the result of poor choices, rather than systemic factors. This is a classic example of the **backfire effect**, a psychological phenomenon in which an attempt to change someone’s beliefs ends up strengthening them instead. It is a stark reminder that good intentions are not enough; the design of a social impact game must be grounded in a deep understanding of human psychology, or risk doing more harm than good.

Another key psychological principle at play in social impact games is **motivation**. What is it that drives us to spend hours upon hours leveling up our characters, collecting virtual treasures, and completing meaningless quests? And can that same motivational power be harnessed for good? The answer, of course, is yes. Games like “Free Rice” and “Charity Miles” are masters of **gamified altruism**. By rewarding players with a sense of accomplishment and a tangible real-world impact, these games tap into our innate desire to do good. The trivia questions in “Free Rice” are not particularly challenging, and the amount of rice donated for each correct answer is minuscule. But the cumulative effect of millions of players answering millions of questions is a powerful force for good. And the simple act of turning a daily walk into a fundraising opportunity, as “Charity Miles” does, can transform a mundane activity into a meaningful one. These games are a testament to the power of small, incremental actions, and a reminder that sometimes the most effective way to change the world is to make it feel like a game.

_REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES_

The world of social impact games is a veritable menagerie of creative and often quirky attempts to tackle some of the world’s most pressing problems. From saving the bees to surviving homelessness, there is a game for almost every cause imaginable. And while the quality and effectiveness of these games vary wildly, they all share a common goal: to make a difference.

Take, for example, **“APICO,”** a game that is, on the surface, a charming and relaxing beekeeping simulator. But beneath the cute pixel art and soothing soundtrack is a serious message about the importance of bee conservation. A portion of the game’s profits are donated to beekeeping and conservation charities, and the game itself is a gentle and engaging introduction to the vital role that bees play in our ecosystem. It is a perfect example of how a game can be both entertaining and educational, without sacrificing either.

Then there is **“Alba: A Wildlife Adventure,”** a game that takes the concept of environmental activism and turns it into a delightful open-world adventure. As the titular Alba, players explore a Mediterranean island, documenting its wildlife and cleaning up its litter. For every copy of the game sold, the developers have pledged to plant a tree. It is a simple but powerful gesture that has resulted in the planting of over a million trees. The game is a testament to the power of collective action, and a reminder that even the smallest of us can make a big difference.

But not all social impact games are so lighthearted. **“CHANGE: A Homeless Survival Experience”** is a gritty and unflinching look at the realities of life on the streets. The game, which was inspired by the developers’ own brush with homelessness, is a brutal and often frustrating experience. But it is also a powerful tool for building empathy and raising awareness about a social issue that is too often ignored. Twenty percent of the game’s profits are donated to organizations that are working to combat homelessness, making it a game that is not only about change, but is also actively creating it.

And then there is **“ICED: I Can End Deportation,”** a game that puts players in the shoes of an undocumented immigrant who is facing deportation. The game is a harrowing and eye-opening experience that highlights the injustices of the immigration system. It is a bold and unapologetic piece of political art, a game that is not afraid to take a stand. And with partnerships with over 100 student and community groups, it is a game that has reached millions of people, sparking conversations and inspiring action.

These are just a few examples of the many social impact games that are out there, each one a unique and creative attempt to use the power of play to make the world a better place. They are a testament to the ingenuity and passion of a new generation of game developers, and a sign that the future of gaming is about more than just high scores and bragging rights.

_DISCUSSION_

So what are we to make of all this? Are social impact games the saviors of humanity, the silver bullet that will finally solve all of our problems? Or are they just a clever new form of propaganda, a way for non-profits and corporations to sell us their agendas under the guise of entertainment? The truth, as is so often the case, lies somewhere in between. The rise of social impact games has profound implications for how we think about everything from consumer behavior to social change, and it raises some thorny ethical questions that we are only just beginning to grapple with.

For the savvy marketer, the lessons from social impact games are clear: if you want to sell someone something, make it feel like a game. The same psychological principles that can be used to encourage people to donate to charity or conserve electricity can also be used to sell them sneakers and soda. The line between gamified altruism and gamified consumerism is a blurry one, and it is a line that is being gleefully exploited by corporations around the world. The loyalty programs, the daily challenges, the virtual badges—it’s all part of a grand, and highly effective, game to win our hearts, our minds, and our wallets.

But the implications of social impact games go far beyond the realm of consumerism. These games have the potential to be a powerful force for social change, to mobilize and engage a generation of young people who are increasingly disillusioned with traditional forms of activism. They can be a gateway to a deeper understanding of complex social issues, a way to spark conversations and inspire action. But they can also be a form of “slacktivism,” a way for us to feel like we are making a difference without actually having to do anything. It is a question that we must all ask ourselves: is clicking a button on a screen the same as marching in the streets? Is donating a few virtual grains of rice a substitute for real-world engagement?

And what of the future of play? As the line between entertainment and social impact continues to blur, we are likely to see a new generation of games that are both deeply engaging and deeply meaningful. The games of the future will not just be about escaping reality; they will be about changing it. They will be about collaborating with others to solve real-world problems, about using the power of play to create a better world. It is a future that is both exciting and a little bit terrifying. A future in which the fate of the world may very well depend on our ability to level up.

_CONCLUSION_

In the grand, often-bewildering casino of life, it appears that the house has a new game. Social impact games, in all their quirky, earnest, and occasionally misguided glory, represent a fascinating new chapter in the long and storied history of play. We have seen how these digital diversions can, with a deft application of psychological jujitsu, nudge us toward better behavior, open our eyes to new perspectives, and even, in some cases, save lives. We have also seen how they can backfire, reinforcing the very biases they seek to dismantle.

So, are these games a panacea for the world’s ills? Of course not. That would be ridiculous. But they are a powerful reminder that play is not just a frivolous escape from reality; it is a fundamental part of how we learn, how we connect, and how we grow. And in a world that is increasingly complex and divided, the idea that we can play our way to a better future is, at the very least, a 1-Up mushroom worth grabbing.
references:
Bassanelli, S., Vasta, N., Bucchiarone, A., & Marconi, A. (2022). Gamification for behavior change: A scientometric review. *Acta Psychologica*, *228*, 103657. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2022.103657

Chaos Theory Games. (2023, March 28). *13 games that non-profits are using to make an impact*. https://www.chaostheorygames.com/blog/13-games-that-non-profits-are-using-to-make-an-impact-fundraising-charity-gaming

Kaufman, G., & Flanagan, M. (2016). Playing the system: Comparing the efficacy and impact of a social justice game. *Cyberpsychology: Journal of Psychosocial Research on Cyberspace*, *10*(2), Article 1. https://doi.org/10.5817/CP2016-2-4

Pappas, S. (2019, September). Games with impact. *Monitor on Psychology*, *50*(8). https://www.apa.org/monitor/2019/09/games-impact
